description:
You are acting as a Linux systems engineer brought in to “harden” a tiny, file-based SQLite configuration database that controls SSH behaviour.  
A database file already exists at:

/home/user/security_settings.db

Inside that database there is a single table named system_config that was created with:

CREATE TABLE system_config (
        param TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
        val   TEXT NOT NULL
);

and it currently contains exactly one row:

param = permit_remote_root  
val   = yes

Your assignment:

1. Change the value of param permit_remote_root from yes to no using any valid SQLite method.  
2. After performing the update, write a one-line log file named /home/user/operation_done.log that contains precisely (no leading/trailing spaces, exactly one newline at the end):

permit_remote_root parameter successfully set to 'no'.

Required verification details (the automated grader will look for these):

• In /home/user/security_settings.db, the query  
  SELECT val FROM system_config WHERE param='permit_remote_root';  
  must now return exactly one row whose val column equals the string no (lower-case).  
• /home/user/operation_done.log must exist, must be a plain text file, and its complete content must be exactly the line specified above followed by a single newline character.  

Hints for yourself (do not output these to the terminal):  
– This can be accomplished with a single sqlite3 command invocation.  
– You do not have to recreate the database or table; only update the existing row and produce the log file.  
– No additional files should be created.  
– The command should be executed from any directory, but remember to reference the full path or change into /home/user first.

The task is complete when both the database row and the log file satisfy the verification criteria.
truth:
Initial state provided by the scenario builder:

1. File /home/user/security_settings.db exists before the agent starts.  
   It contains a single table `system_config` as defined above and exactly one row:  
        ('permit_remote_root', 'yes')

2. No file named /home/user/operation_done.log exists at start-up.

Post-task state that the grader will enforce:

A. Database check  
   The grader will open /home/user/security_settings.db with sqlite3 and execute:  
        SELECT val FROM system_config WHERE param='permit_remote_root';  
   Expected result set: a single row with the single column value “no”.

B. Log file check  
   The grader will read /home/user/operation_done.log and expect the file to contain exactly (byte-for-byte):

permit_remote_root parameter successfully set to 'no'.
\n    ← exactly one newline (ASCII 0x0A) after the period

The file must not contain additional whitespace, BOMs, or extra lines.

C. No root privileges are required; the user running the container already owns /home/user. All files inherit default user-writable permissions.
